# "Attention Is All You Need" (arXiv:1706.03762)

## What it is about

This paper introduces the **Transformer**, a novel neural network architecture for sequence transduction (e.g., machine translation). The key idea is that the architecture relies **solely on attention mechanisms**, completely dispensing with recurrence (RNNs/LSTMs) and convolutions (CNNs), which were the dominant building blocks for sequence models at the time.

## Main Contribution

The Transformer architecture, which uses **self-attention** as its core computational mechanism in an encoder-decoder configuration. The authors demonstrate that this approach is:

1. **Superior in translation quality** — achieving **28.4 BLEU** on WMT 2014 English-to-German (improving over the previous best, including ensembles, by over 2 BLEU) and a new single-model state-of-the-art of **41.8 BLEU** on WMT 2014 English-to-French.
2. **More parallelizable** — since there is no sequential recurrence, training can be parallelized far more effectively.
3. **Much faster to train** — the English-to-French result was achieved after training for only **3.5 days on eight GPUs**, a small fraction of the cost of competing models.
4. **Generalizable** — they also show it works well on **English constituency parsing**, demonstrating the architecture's applicability beyond machine translation.

## Key Facts

- **Authors:** Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Lukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin (8 authors)
- **Originally submitted:** June 12, 2017
- **Subjects:** Computation and Language (cs.CL), Machine Learning (cs.LG)
- **Paper:** 15 pages, 5 figures

This paper became one of the most influential papers in modern deep learning, as the Transformer architecture became the foundation for virtually all large language models (BERT, GPT, etc.) and many other models across NLP, vision, and beyond.
"""

# Postage Stamp — Wikipedia Article Summary

The Wikipedia article on **Postage Stamp** describes the small piece of paper issued by a postal authority as evidence of payment of postage. Here are the key facts from that article:

## Definition & Purpose
- A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper purchased and displayed on an item of mail as evidence that postage has been paid.
- It is typically affixed to the envelope or package by the sender.

## History
- The first adhesive postage stamp was the **Penny Black**, issued by **Great Britain** on **May 1, 1840**, bearing the image of **Queen Victoria**.
- The reform was driven by **Sir Rowland Hill**, who proposed a uniform penny post system in his 1837 pamphlet *Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability*.
- Before adhesive stamps, postal systems used various methods including hand-stamping, prepaid letter sheets, and the recipient often paid on delivery.
- The **Two Penny Blue** was issued shortly after the Penny Black.
- **Brazil** was among the first countries to follow, issuing the **Bull's Eye stamps** in 1843. **Switzerland** (Zurich and Geneva cantonals) and the **United States** (1847) followed soon after.

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — common, everyday use, produced in large quantities
- **Commemorative stamps** — issued to honor people, events, or subjects, usually for a limited time
- **Airmail stamps** — for mail carried by air
- **Postage due stamps** — indicating insufficient postage was paid
- **Revenue/fiscal stamps** — for tax purposes (distinct but related)
- **Semi-postal (charity) stamps** — include a surcharge for charitable causes
- **Miniature sheets/souvenir sheets**
- **Coil stamps, booklet stamps**

## Design & Production
- Stamps are printed using methods such as **engraving (intaglio)**, **lithography**, **typography**, and **photogravure**.
- **Perforations** were introduced (first used in Britain in 1854) to allow easy separation of stamps from sheets.
- Stamps feature **denominations**, the **issuing country's name** (except Great Britain, which uses the monarch's silhouette instead), and artwork.
- **Watermarks** and other security features are used to prevent counterfeiting.
- **Gum** (adhesive) is applied to the back; **self-adhesive** stamps became common in later decades.

## Stamp Collecting (Philately)
- **Philately** is the study and collection of stamps and postal history.
- It is one of the world's most popular hobbies.
- Stamps are collected based on condition: **mint** (unused, with original gum) and **used** (cancelled/postmarked).
- Key considerations include **centering**, **perforations**, **hinge marks**, and **cancellation quality**.
- Notable rarities include the **British Guiana 1c Magenta**, the **Inverted Jenny** (US 1918 airmail error), and the **Treskilling Yellow** of Sweden.

## Modern Developments
- Digital postage and declining letter mail have affected stamp use.
- Many postal authorities issue stamps primarily for **collectors** (a revenue source).
- Some countries have introduced **QR codes**, **digital stamps**, and **crypto stamps**.
- Self-adhesive stamps have largely replaced lick-and-stick (water-activated gum) stamps in many countries.

## Notable Facts
- The **Universal Postal Union (UPU)**, founded in 1874, requires member nations to print their country name on stamps in Latin script (Great Britain is exempt as the originator of adhesive stamps).
- The study of stamps can provide insights into a country's **history, culture, politics, and art**.

This covers the principal content and key facts from the Wikipedia article on postage stamps.

# arXiv:1706.03762 — "Attention Is All You Need"

This is one of the most influential papers in modern deep learning. Here are the key facts:

## Authors
Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin. The work was primarily from Google Brain and Google Research, with a University of Toronto affiliation (Gomez).

## Published
Presented at **NeurIPS 2017** (then called NIPS).

## What It's About
The paper introduces the **Transformer** architecture, a novel sequence-to-sequence model for neural machine translation (and other sequence transduction tasks) that relies **entirely on attention mechanisms**, dispensing with recurrence (RNNs/LSTMs) and convolutions altogether.

## Main Contributions

1. **The Transformer Architecture**: Built entirely on **multi-head self-attention** and position-wise feed-forward layers. It uses an encoder-decoder structure where both encoder and decoder are stacks of identical layers.

2. **Multi-Head Attention**: Rather than a single attention function, the model projects queries, keys, and values into multiple subspaces (heads) and attends in parallel, then concatenates the results.

3. **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**: The attention function computes dot products of queries and keys, scaled by $1/\sqrt{d_k}$, followed by softmax and multiplication with values.

4. **Positional Encoding**: Since the model has no recurrence or convolution, it injects positional information using sinusoidal functions of different frequencies added to the input embeddings.

5. **Superior Performance with Greater Parallelizability**: The model achieved **state-of-the-art results** on WMT 2014 English-to-German (28.4 BLEU) and English-to-French (41.0 BLEU) translation benchmarks, while requiring significantly **less training time** than previous best models because attention operations are highly parallelizable (unlike sequential RNN computation).

## Key Architectural Details
- The base model uses $d_{model} = 512$, 6 encoder layers, 6 decoder layers, 8 attention heads, and feed-forward inner dimension of 2048.
- The decoder uses **masked self-attention** to prevent positions from attending to future tokens (preserving autoregressive generation).
- Residual connections and layer normalization are applied around each sub-layer.
- Label smoothing and the Adam optimizer with a custom warm-up learning rate schedule (the "Noam" schedule) were used.

## Impact
This paper is the foundation of virtually all modern large language models (BERT, GPT, T5, PaLM, LLaMA, etc.) and has been cited tens of thousands of times. The Transformer has become the dominant architecture far beyond NLP, extending into vision (ViT), audio, protein structure prediction (AlphaFold), and many other domains.
"""

# "Attention Is All You Need" — Vaswani et al. (2017)

The URL points to the landmark paper **"Attention Is All You Need"** by Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin, published at NeurIPS 2017.

## What It Is

This paper introduces the **Transformer** architecture — a sequence-to-sequence model that dispenses entirely with recurrence and convolutions, relying solely on **attention mechanisms** to draw global dependencies between input and output.

## Key Facts and Contributions

### Architecture
- **Encoder-decoder structure**, each composed of stacked identical layers.
- **Encoder**: 6 identical layers, each with two sub-layers:
  1. **Multi-head self-attention**
  2. **Position-wise feed-forward network** (two linear transformations with a ReLU in between, inner dimension 2048)
- **Decoder**: 6 identical layers, each with three sub-layers — the two above plus a **multi-head cross-attention** layer attending to the encoder output. The self-attention in the decoder is **masked** to prevent attending to future positions.
- **Residual connections** around each sub-layer, followed by **layer normalization**.

### Scaled Dot-Product Attention
$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$
- Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents dot products from growing large and pushing softmax into regions of extremely small gradients.

### Multi-Head Attention
- Instead of a single attention function, queries, keys, and values are linearly projected **h times** (h = 8 heads) into $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h = 64$-dimensional subspaces.
- Attention is applied in parallel across heads; outputs are concatenated and projected.
- This allows the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions.

### Positional Encoding
- Since the model has no recurrence or convolution, **sinusoidal positional encodings** are added to input embeddings to inject information about token position:
  - $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$
  - $PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$
- Learned positional embeddings were also tested and found to give nearly identical results.

### Model Dimensions (Base / Big)
| Parameter | Base | Big |
|---|---|---|
| $d_{\text{model}}$ | 512 | 1024 |
| $h$ (heads) | 8 | 16 |
| $d_{ff}$ | 2048 | 4096 |
| Layers (N) | 6 | 6 |
| Parameters | ~65M | ~213M |

### Training Details
- **Dataset**: WMT 2014 English-German (~4.5M sentence pairs) and WMT 2014 English-French (~36M sentence pairs).
- **Optimizer**: Adam with $\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.98$, $\epsilon=10^{-9}$.
- **Learning rate schedule**: The famous "warmup then decay" schedule — linear warmup for 4000 steps, then decay proportional to the inverse square root of the step number.
- **Regularization**: Dropout (0.1 for base, 0.3 for big), label smoothing ($\epsilon_{ls}=0.1$).
- Trained on 8 NVIDIA P100 GPUs; base model for ~12 hours, big model for ~3.5 days.

### Results
- **English-German**: 28.4 BLEU (big model) — new state of the art at the time, surpassing all previous single models and ensembles.
- **English-French**: 41.0 BLEU (big model) — state of the art, achieved at a fraction of the training cost of previous best models.
- Key advantage: **much lower computational cost** compared to recurrent or convolutional models, due to the parallelizability of attention.

### Why It Matters
- Self-attention has $O(1)$ sequential operations (vs. $O(n)$ for RNNs), enabling far greater parallelism.
- Maximum path length between any two positions is $O(1)$ (vs. $O(n)$ for RNNs, $O(\log n)$ for dilated convolutions), facilitating learning of long-range dependencies.
- This architecture became the foundation for virtually all subsequent large language models (BERT, GPT, T5, PaLM, LLaMA, etc.) and revolutionized NLP and beyond (vision, audio, biology, etc.).
"""

# Postage Stamp — Wikipedia Article Summary

The Wikipedia article on **Postage Stamp** describes the small piece of paper issued by a postal authority that serves as evidence of prepayment of postage. Here are the key facts covered in the article:

## Definition & Purpose
A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper purchased and displayed on an item of mail as evidence that postage has been paid. The stamp is typically affixed to the envelope or package, and the postal service cancels it (marks it) to prevent reuse.

## History
- The first adhesive postage stamp was the **Penny Black**, issued by **Great Britain** on **May 1, 1840**, as part of postal reforms championed by **Sir Rowland Hill**.
- Before adhesive stamps, postal systems used various methods including the recipient paying upon delivery, hand-stamped markings, and prepaid letter sheets.
- Rowland Hill proposed a uniform penny post with prepaid adhesive labels in his 1837 pamphlet *"Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability."*
- The Penny Black featured a profile of **Queen Victoria** based on a medallion by William Wyon.
- **Brazil** was the second country to issue stamps (the **Bull's Eye** stamps, 1843), followed by **Zurich** (also 1843) and the **United States** (1847).

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — common, everyday stamps for standard postage
- **Commemorative stamps** — issued to honor people, events, or subjects
- **Airmail stamps**
- **Postage due stamps**
- **Revenue stamps** (for tax purposes)
- **Semi-postal/charity stamps** — include a surcharge for charitable causes
- **Miniature sheets/souvenir sheets**

## Design & Production
- Stamps are printed using methods such as **engraving (intaglio)**, **lithography**, **photogravure**, and **offset printing**.
- They typically include the **country name** (the UPU requires this, with Great Britain being the sole exception — it uses the monarch's silhouette instead), **denomination**, and imagery.
- **Perforations** were introduced in 1854 (by Henry Archer's machine) to allow easy separation of stamps from sheets.
- Stamps may include **watermarks** and other security features.

## Philately
- **Philately** is the study and collection of stamps, and it is one of the world's most popular hobbies.
- Stamp collecting emerged almost immediately after stamps were first issued.
- Rare stamps can be extraordinarily valuable; examples include the **British Guiana 1c Magenta**, the **Treskilling Yellow**, and the **Inverted Jenny**.

## Usage & Decline
- With the rise of digital communications, email, and electronic payment methods, the volume of physical mail has declined.
- Many postal services now use **meter stamps**, **permit mail**, and **digital/printed postage** as alternatives.
- Despite declining postal use, stamps continue to be produced for collectors and remain culturally significant.

## Stamp Catalogs
The article references major catalogs used by collectors, including **Scott**, **Stanley Gibbons**, **Michel**, and **Yvert et Tellier**.

6. **Distillation**: The paper also presented distilled smaller models (based on Qwen and Llama architectures, ranging from 1.5B to 70B parameters) that were fine-tuned on reasoning data generated by DeepSeek-R1, showing that smaller models could acquire strong reasoning capabilities through distillation.

7. **Base model**: DeepSeek-R1 was built on top of DeepSeek-V3, their mixture-of-experts (MoE) base model.

# Paper: "Reasoning Language Models: A Blueprint" (arXiv:2501.12948)

This paper, authored by Maciej Besta, Julia Barber, and others from ETH Zurich (with contributions from other institutions), published in January 2025, provides a comprehensive survey and blueprint/taxonomy for **Reasoning Language Models (RLMs)** — also sometimes called Large Reasoning Models (LRMs). These are systems like OpenAI's o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1, and similar models that go beyond standard LLM inference by performing explicit, structured reasoning at test time.

## Key Facts and Contributions

### Core Concept
The paper distinguishes between standard **Large Language Models (LLMs)** that produce answers in a single forward pass (or with simple prompting like chain-of-thought) and **Reasoning Language Models (RLMs)** that explicitly search through a space of reasoning steps/thoughts during inference, often using tree or graph structures.

### Taxonomy / Blueprint
The paper proposes a structured blueprint that decomposes reasoning systems along several dimensions:

1. **Reasoning Structure**: How the reasoning trace is organized:
   - **Chains** (linear sequences of thoughts, as in Chain-of-Thought)
   - **Trees** (branching reasoning paths, as in Tree-of-Thoughts)
   - **Graphs** (DAG-like structures where reasoning steps can merge/reference each other)

2. **Reasoning Generation / Expansion**: How new reasoning steps (thoughts) are generated — e.g., using the LLM itself to propose next steps, or using specialized modules.

3. **Reasoning Evaluation / Verification**: How candidate reasoning steps or final answers are scored:
   - **Process Reward Models (PRMs)** — score intermediate steps
   - **Outcome Reward Models (ORMs)** — score final answers
   - Self-evaluation by the LLM itself
   - Other verifiers

4. **Search Algorithms**: How the space of reasoning traces is explored:
   - Beam search
   - Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)
   - Best-first search
   - Other strategies

5. **Training / Learning**: How the model learns to reason better:
   - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** — e.g., PPO, GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization, as used in DeepSeek-R1)
   - **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** on reasoning traces (distillation)
   - **Self-play** and **self-improvement** loops
   - Online vs. offline RL

### Key Systems Analyzed
The paper contextualizes and analyzes several notable systems within its framework:
- **OpenAI o1 and o3** — use internal chain-of-thought reasoning with RL training
- **DeepSeek-R1** — uses GRPO for RL training, demonstrates emergent reasoning behaviors (like self-verification, "aha moments")
- **STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner)** and variants
- **Tree-of-Thoughts**, **Graph-of-Thoughts**
- **AlphaProof / AlphaGeometry** (DeepMind's math reasoning systems)
- **Journey Learning**, **Quiet-STaR**, and many others

### Test-Time Compute Scaling
A major theme is the idea of **scaling test-time compute** — rather than only scaling model parameters or training data, you can improve performance by spending more computation during inference (searching more reasoning paths, doing more verification steps). The paper discusses how this represents a new scaling axis complementary to traditional scaling laws.

### Key Insights
- There is a spectrum from **implicit reasoning** (internalized in weights via training) to **explicit reasoning** (visible reasoning traces at inference time)
- Process reward models (PRMs) that evaluate intermediate steps tend to be more effective than outcome-only reward models for guiding search
- RL-based training can lead to **emergent reasoning behaviors** not explicitly programmed
- The paper emphasizes the importance of **verification** as a key component — it's often easier to verify a solution than to generate one, which is leveraged by these systems

### Scope
The paper covers reasoning in domains including:
- Mathematical problem-solving
- Code generation
- Logical reasoning
- Planning
- Scientific reasoning

## What the URL "asks me to do"

Since the paper is a survey/blueprint, "doing what it describes" means understanding and being able to articulate this taxonomy of reasoning language models. I've provided the key framework and facts above. The paper essentially provides a unifying lens to understand the rapidly evolving landscape of LLM reasoning systems as of early 2025.
"""

- **Edges** connect two distinct vertices if and only if they **commute**

# Postage Stamp — Summary of the Wikipedia Article

The Wikipedia article on **Postage Stamp** covers the history, purpose, production, and collecting of postage stamps. Here are the key facts:

## Definition
A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper issued by a postal authority for attachment to mail, indicating that postage has been paid or partially paid. The cost of the stamp is the fee for the postal service to deliver the item.

## History
- **The Penny Black**, issued on **May 1, 1840**, in the **United Kingdom**, was the **world's first adhesive postage stamp** used in a public postal system. It featured a profile of **Queen Victoria**.
- The system was proposed by **Sir Rowland Hill** as part of his postal reforms, outlined in his 1837 pamphlet *Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability*. Hill advocated for a uniform penny postage rate regardless of distance within the UK.
- Before stamps, postage was typically paid by the recipient, and rates varied by distance.
- The **Penny Red** replaced the Penny Black in 1841 because the black ink made cancellation marks hard to see.
- Other countries quickly adopted stamps: **Brazil** (1843, the "Bull's Eyes"), **Switzerland**, and the **United States** (1847, featuring Benjamin Franklin and George Washington).

## Design and Production
- Stamps are typically printed on specially manufactured **gummed paper** with **perforations** for easy separation (perforations were introduced in 1854).
- Early stamps were **imperforate** and had to be cut with scissors.
- Common security features include **watermarks**, special paper, and intricate engraved designs to prevent counterfeiting.
- Stamps may be **definitive** (standard, everyday use, printed in large quantities) or **commemorative** (issued for limited periods to honor people, events, or subjects).

## Elements of a Stamp
- Country name (the UK is uniquely exempt from this requirement by UPU convention, instead using the monarch's silhouette)
- Denomination (face value)
- An illustration or design

## Stamp Collecting (Philately)
- **Philately** is the study and collection of stamps.
- It is one of the world's most popular hobbies, dating back to the 1860s.
- Stamps are valued based on rarity, condition, centering, gum quality, and historical significance.
- Notable rare stamps include the **British Guiana 1c Magenta** (one of the world's rarest and most valuable stamps), the **Inverted Jenny** (a U.S. stamp with an upside-down airplane), and the **Treskilling Yellow** from Sweden.

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — everyday use
- **Commemorative stamps** — special occasions
- **Airmail stamps** — for air-delivered mail
- **Postage due stamps** — indicating insufficient postage
- **Semi-postal (charity) stamps** — include a surcharge for a charitable cause
- **Revenue stamps** — for tax purposes (distinct from postage stamps)
- **Self-adhesive stamps** — modern stamps with peel-off backing, largely replacing lick-and-stick gummed stamps

## Modern Usage
- With the decline of traditional mail, many postal authorities issue stamps primarily for **collectors** and as **cultural commemorations**.
- Some countries have introduced **digital stamps** or **QR code-based** postage.
- **Forever stamps** (notably in the U.S., introduced in 2007) remain valid regardless of future rate increases.

## The Universal Postal Union (UPU)
- Founded in **1874**, the UPU coordinates postal policies among member nations, including standards for stamp issuance.

This article serves as a comprehensive overview of the postage stamp's role in postal history, commerce, and culture.

# arXiv:2601.03267 — OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

## What it is
This is the **system card** published by OpenAI alongside the launch of **GPT-5 in August 2025**. It was submitted to arXiv on **19 December 2025** (v1), with a revision on **1 May 2026** (v2, adding monitorability evals and authors). It has **485 authors** and falls under cs.CL and cs.AI.

## Key/Headline Claims

1. **Unified system with a router:** GPT-5 is a unified system comprising:
   - A **smart and fast model** for most questions
   - A **deeper reasoning model** (gpt-5-thinking) for harder problems
   - A **real-time router** that decides which model to invoke based on conversation type, complexity, tool needs, and explicit user intent (e.g., "think hard about this")
   - Once usage limits are hit, a **mini version** of each model handles remaining queries

2. **Router continuously improves:** The router is trained on real signals including user model-switching behavior, preference rates, and measured correctness.

3. **Outperforms previous models** on benchmarks, answers questions more quickly, and — they emphasize — is **more useful for real-world queries**.

4. **Specific improvements:**
   - Significant reductions in **hallucinations**
   - Improved **instruction following**
   - Minimized **sycophancy**
   - Leveled-up performance in ChatGPT's three most common use cases: **writing, coding, and health**

5. **Safe-completions:** All GPT-5 models feature "safe-completions," described as their latest approach to safety training to prevent disallowed content.

6. **Preparedness Framework — Biological/Chemical risk:** They treat **gpt-5-thinking** as **High capability** in the Biological and Chemical domain (same classification as ChatGPT agent), activating associated safeguards. They note this is a **precautionary** decision — they do not have definitive evidence the model could meaningfully help a novice create severe biological harm (their threshold for "High"), but chose to err on the side of caution.

7. **System card focus:** The document focuses primarily on **gpt-5-thinking** and **gpt-5-main**, with evaluations for other models in the appendix.
"""

# Postage Stamp — Summary of the Wikipedia Article

The Wikipedia article on **Postage stamp** covers the history, purpose, production, and collecting of postage stamps. Here are the key facts:

## Definition
A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper issued by a postal authority to be affixed to mail as evidence of prepayment of postage. The sender purchases stamps and places them on the envelope or package, indicating that the appropriate fee for delivery has been paid.

## History
- **The Penny Black**, issued on **May 1, 1840**, in **Great Britain**, was the **world's first adhesive postage stamp** used in a public postal system. It featured a profile of **Queen Victoria**.
- The idea is credited to **Sir Rowland Hill**, who proposed postal reform in his 1837 pamphlet *"Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability."* He advocated for a uniform penny postage rate regardless of distance within Britain.
- Before stamps, postage was typically paid by the recipient, and rates varied by distance, which was cumbersome and expensive.
- The **Penny Red** replaced the Penny Black in 1841 because the black ink made cancellation marks hard to see.
- Other countries quickly adopted stamps: **Brazil** (1843, the "Bull's Eyes"), **Switzerland**, and the **United States** (1847, featuring Benjamin Franklin and George Washington).

## Design and Production
- Stamps are typically printed on special paper, often with security features like **watermarks**, **perforations**, and **phosphor tagging** (for automated mail sorting).
- **Perforations** were introduced in 1854 in Britain (invented by **Henry Archer**) to allow stamps to be easily separated.
- Common subjects include heads of state, national symbols, famous people, historical events, flora, fauna, and commemorative themes.
- **Definitive stamps** are standard, everyday-use stamps produced in large quantities; **commemorative stamps** are issued for limited periods to honor events or people.

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — standard/regular issue
- **Commemorative stamps** — special occasions
- **Airmail stamps** — for airmail service
- **Postage due stamps** — for underpaid mail
- **Revenue stamps** — for tax purposes (related but distinct)
- **Semi-postal (charity) stamps** — include a surcharge for charitable causes
- **Miniature sheets / souvenir sheets**

## Stamp Collecting (Philately)
- Stamp collecting is one of the world's most popular hobbies, known as **philately**.
- Key aspects include condition, rarity, centering, cancellation type, and gum condition.
- Rare stamps like the **British Guiana 1c Magenta** (1856), the **Inverted Jenny** (US, 1918), and the **Treskilling Yellow** (Sweden, 1855) are among the most valuable in the world.

## Modern Developments
- Many countries now use **self-adhesive stamps** instead of lick-and-stick gummed stamps.
- **Forever stamps** (notably in the US, introduced 2007) remain valid regardless of future rate increases.
- Digital postage and declining letter mail have reduced stamp usage, but stamps continue to be issued, often partly for collector revenue.
- Some postal services have introduced **personalized/customizable stamps** where customers can add their own images.

## Cultural Significance
- Stamps serve as miniature works of art and cultural ambassadors, reflecting a nation's identity, history, and values.
- They have been used for **propaganda** purposes by various governments.

This article is one of the comprehensive reference articles on Wikipedia covering postal history, philatelic terminology, and the evolution of mail systems worldwide.
"""

## Definition
A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper issued by a postal authority to be affixed to mail as evidence of payment of postage (prepayment for postal services).

## History
- The first adhesive postage stamp was the **Penny Black**, issued by **Great Britain** on **May 1, 1840**, bearing the image of **Queen Victoria**.
- The concept is credited to **Sir Rowland Hill**, who proposed postal reform in his 1837 pamphlet *Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability*.
- Before stamps, postage was typically paid by the recipient, and rates were based on distance and number of sheets.
- Hill's reform introduced a uniform penny rate regardless of distance within the UK.
- The **Two Penny Blue** was issued shortly after the Penny Black.
- Other countries quickly adopted the idea: **Brazil** (1843, the Bull's Eye stamps), **Switzerland**, and the **United States** (1847, featuring Benjamin Franklin and George Washington).

## Production
- Stamps are typically printed on special paper, often with **watermarks** and **perforations** (introduced in 1854 by Henry Archer) to allow easy separation.
- Printing methods include **engraving (intaglio)**, **lithography**, **typography**, and **photogravure**.
- **Gum** is applied to the back for adhesive purposes; **self-adhesive** stamps became widespread in later decades.

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — standard everyday stamps, produced in large quantities
- **Commemorative stamps** — issued to honor events, people, or anniversaries, usually for limited periods
- **Airmail stamps**
- **Postage due stamps** — for underpaid mail
- **Revenue stamps** — for tax purposes (though distinct from postage stamps)
- **Semi-postal (charity) stamps** — include a surcharge for charitable causes
- **Miniature sheets / souvenir sheets**

- Stamp collecting is one of the world's most popular hobbies.
- The study of stamps is called **philately**.
- Rare stamps can be extremely valuable (e.g., the **British Guiana 1c Magenta**, the **Treskilling Yellow** of Sweden, the **Inverted Jenny** of the US).

## Design and Subjects
- Early stamps primarily depicted monarchs or national symbols.
- Modern stamps feature a wide variety of subjects: historical figures, flora/fauna, art, sports, cultural events, etc.
- The **Universal Postal Union (UPU)**, established in 1874, helped standardize international mail and stamp usage.

## Notable Facts
- **Great Britain** is the only country that does not include its name on its stamps, instead using the monarch's silhouette, a privilege as the originator of the postage stamp.
- The transition from traditional gummed stamps to **self-adhesive** stamps accelerated in the late 20th and early 21st centuries.
- **Phosphor bands** or **tagging** was introduced to allow automated mail sorting.

# Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces

**arXiv ID:** 2312.00752

**Authors:** Albert Gu and Tri Dao

**What it introduces:**

Mamba is a novel sequence modeling architecture based on **selective state space models (SSMs)**. Its key ideas and facts are:

1. **Selective State Spaces:** The core innovation is making the SSM parameters **input-dependent** (functions of the input), allowing the model to **selectively propagate or forget information** along the sequence based on the current token. This addresses a key weakness of prior SSMs — their inability to perform **content-based reasoning**, which was critical for discrete modalities like language.

2. **Hardware-Aware Parallel Algorithm:** Because making SSM parameters input-dependent breaks the ability to use efficient convolutions, the authors designed a **hardware-aware parallel algorithm in recurrent mode** to maintain computational efficiency.

3. **Simplified Architecture:** Mamba is a simplified end-to-end neural network architecture that uses **no attention and no MLP blocks** — it relies entirely on selective SSMs as its core building block.

4. **Key Performance Properties:**
   - **Linear-time scaling** in sequence length (as opposed to Transformers' quadratic attention)
   - **5× higher inference throughput** than Transformers
   - Performance scales up to **million-length sequences**

5. **Results:** Achieves state-of-the-art performance across **language, audio, and genomics**. The **Mamba-3B** model outperforms Transformers of the same size and matches Transformers **twice its size** in both pretraining and downstream evaluation on language modeling.

**Submitted:** December 1, 2023 (v1); revised May 31, 2024 (v2).

# Postage Stamp — Wikipedia Article Summary

The Wikipedia article on **Postage Stamp** describes what a postage stamp is and covers its history, production, types, and related topics. Here are the key facts:

## Definition
A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper issued by a postal authority for attachment to mail, serving as evidence of prepayment of postage. It is typically affixed to the envelope or package.

## History
- **The Penny Black**, issued on **May 1, 1840**, in **Great Britain**, was the **world's first adhesive postage stamp** used in a public postal system. It featured a profile of **Queen Victoria**.
- The concept is credited to **Sir Rowland Hill**, who proposed postal reform in his 1837 pamphlet *"Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability."* He advocated for a uniform penny postage rate regardless of distance within the UK.
- Before stamps, postage was typically paid by the recipient, and rates varied by distance.
- The **Two Penny Blue** was issued shortly after the Penny Black.
- Other countries quickly adopted the idea: **Brazil** (1843, the "Bull's Eye" stamps), **Switzerland**, and the **United States** (1847, featuring Benjamin Franklin and George Washington).

## Elements of a Stamp
- **Denomination** (face value)
- **Country name** (the UK is uniquely exempt from this requirement, using the monarch's silhouette instead, as the originator of the postage stamp)
- **Design/illustration**
- **Perforations** (introduced in the 1850s by Henry Archer to allow easy separation)
- **Gum/adhesive** on the back

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — standard, everyday use, produced in large quantities
- **Commemorative stamps** — issued to honor events, people, or anniversaries
- **Airmail stamps**
- **Postage due stamps**
- **Revenue stamps**
- **Semi-postal (charity) stamps** — include a surcharge for charitable purposes
- **Miniature sheets/souvenir sheets**

## Stamp Collecting (Philately)
- Stamp collecting is one of the world's most popular hobbies, known as **philately**.
- Rare stamps can be extremely valuable (e.g., the **British Guiana 1c Magenta**, the **Treskilling Yellow** from Sweden, the **Inverted Jenny** from the US).

## Production
- Stamps are printed using various methods: **engraving (intaglio)**, **lithography**, **photogravure**, and **typography**.
- Security features are often included to prevent counterfeiting, such as **watermarks**, **phosphor bands**, and special inks.

## Modern Developments
- **Self-adhesive stamps** have largely replaced lick-and-stick gum stamps.
- **Forever stamps** (or non-denominated stamps) remain valid regardless of future rate increases.
- Digital postage and declining letter mail have reduced the functional role of stamps, though they remain culturally significant and collectible.

## Universal Postal Union (UPU)
- The UPU, established in 1874, helps coordinate postal policies between nations, including stamp standards.

These are the principal facts covered in the Wikipedia article on postage stamps.

## Key facts

**Architecture / Idea introduced:** **Mamba**, a new sequence modeling architecture based on **selective state space models (selective SSMs)**.

### Core contributions:

1. **Selective State Spaces (S6):** The paper introduces a **selection mechanism** into structured state space models (SSMs, building on prior work like S4). The key insight is making the SSM parameters **input-dependent** (i.e., the matrices/parameters that govern the state transitions vary depending on the input at each time step). This allows the model to selectively propagate or forget information along the sequence depending on the content — addressing a fundamental limitation of prior SSMs, which were linear time-invariant (LTI) and thus content-independent.

2. **Hardware-aware parallel scan implementation:** Because making the parameters input-dependent breaks the convolutional mode that made prior SSMs efficient, the authors develop a **hardware-aware algorithm** that computes the selective SSM recurrence efficiently on GPUs using a parallel scan, avoiding materializing the full expanded state in HBM (high-bandwidth memory) and instead using SRAM-based kernel fusion.

3. **Simplified architecture:** Mamba uses a streamlined architecture that **removes the attention and MLP blocks** found in Transformers. It integrates the selective SSM into a simple block design (inspired by H3 and gated architectures), stacking these homogeneous blocks to form the full model. There are no attention mechanisms at all.

### Key properties:

- **Linear-time scaling** in sequence length (O(N) rather than O(N²) for attention), enabling efficient processing of very long sequences.
- **Constant memory** during autoregressive inference (true RNN-like recurrence at generation time), leading to much higher throughput.
- Achieves **state-of-the-art results** across language modeling, DNA modeling, and audio, matching or exceeding Transformer performance at various scales (up to ~3B parameters in the paper's experiments).
- On language modeling, Mamba matches Transformer quality with **~5× higher inference throughput** due to the linear-time decoding.

This paper was highly influential in sparking widespread interest in SSM-based architectures as a viable alternative to Transformers.

# Paper: "Beyond Human Data: Scaling Self-Training for Problem-Solving with Language Models" (STaR / ReST^EM)

This is the paper describing **ReST^EM** (Reinforced Self-Training with Expectation-Maximization), published by Google DeepMind researchers (Avi Singh, John D. Co-Reyes, Rishabh Agarwal, Ankesh Anand, Piyush Patil, Peter J. Liu, James Harrison, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, Aaron Parisi, et al.), appearing around December 2023.

## Key Idea

The paper proposes a method to improve large language models on reasoning/problem-solving tasks by iteratively training on self-generated solutions, going **beyond** the limitations of human-generated data. The method is framed as an Expectation-Maximization (EM) procedure:

1. **E-step (Generate):** Sample many candidate solutions from the current model for each problem/question.
2. **M-step (Improve):** Filter to keep only correct solutions (verified by ground-truth answers or a reward signal), then fine-tune the model on this filtered dataset.
3. **Iterate** — repeat the above steps multiple times.

## Key Facts and Contributions

- **Relation to STaR:** ReST^EM is closely related to the STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner) method but differs in that it generates the full dataset once per iteration (offline) rather than online, and frames the process formally as EM.
- **Tasks:** Evaluated primarily on **MATH** (competition mathematics) and **APPS/HumanEval** (code generation) benchmarks.
- **Models:** Uses PaLM 2 models of various sizes as the base models.
- **Scaling self-training beyond human data:** The key finding is that training on model-generated correct solutions can surpass the performance achieved by training on human-written solutions alone. The model improves substantially over multiple iterations.
- **Diminishing returns:** Performance typically improves for ~2-3 iterations of ReST^EM before gains plateau or the model begins to overfit on its own distribution.
- **Pass@1 improvements:** Significant improvements on MATH (e.g., going from ~30s% to ~40s% on PaLM 2-L) and on code generation tasks.
- **Solution diversity decreases** over iterations, which is one reason for the eventual saturation.
- **Simple and scalable:** The method is straightforward to implement — it requires only the ability to verify correctness of solutions (binary reward), not fine-grained reward models.
- **Comparison to other methods:** Outperforms or matches more complex reinforcement learning approaches like online RL methods, while being simpler to implement.

## Summary

The paper demonstrates that LLMs can substantially improve their mathematical reasoning and code generation abilities by repeatedly training on their own correct outputs, formalized as an EM algorithm. This "self-improvement" loop allows models to surpass what's achievable from human demonstration data alone, though gains saturate after a few iterations due to decreasing solution diversity.

# CSS `:has()` Pseudo-class — Baseline Status

Based on the MDN reference content, here are the key facts:

## Baseline Status

- **Baseline 2023 — Newly available**: The `:has()` CSS pseudo-class became **Baseline Newly available** since **December 2023**. As of that date, it works across the latest devices and browser versions.

- **Baseline Widely available**: The content does **not** state that `:has()` has reached Baseline Widely available status. It only carries the "Newly available" designation, with the caveat that "this feature might not work in older devices or browsers."

## Key Facts about `:has()`

- It is a **functional CSS pseudo-class** that represents an element if any of the relative selectors passed as an argument match at least one element when anchored against that element.
- It enables selecting a **parent element** or a **previous sibling element** with respect to a reference element — something not previously possible with CSS selectors alone.
- It takes on the **specificity** of the most specific selector in its arguments (same behavior as `:is()` and `:not()`).
- It **cannot be nested** within another `:has()`.
- **Pseudo-elements** are not valid selectors within `:has()` and are not valid anchors for `:has()`.
- If `:has()` is not supported in a browser, the entire selector block fails unless it is placed in a forgiving selector list (such as within `:is()` or `:where()`).
- The page also includes **performance considerations**, advising developers to avoid broad anchoring (e.g., `body:has(...)`) and to minimize subtree traversals by using constrained combinators like `>` or `+`.

"""
Postage Stamp Problem Implementation

Given a set of stamp denominations and a maximum number of stamps you can use,
determine:
1. Which postage values can be formed
2. The largest value that CANNOT be formed (Frobenius number, for 2 denominations)
3. The minimum number of stamps needed for each value
"""

from math import gcd
from typing import List, Optional, Tuple


def min_stamps(denominations: List[int], max_value: int, max_stamps: Optional[int] = None) -> List[int]:
    """
    For each value from 0..max_value, compute the minimum number of stamps needed.
    Returns a list where result[v] = minimum stamps to make value v, or -1 if impossible.
    
    If max_stamps is given, values requiring more than max_stamps are marked -1.
    """
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_value + 1)
    dp[0] = 0

    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 < dp[v]:
                dp[v] = dp[v - d] + 1

    if max_stamps is not None:
        return [dp[v] if dp[v] <= max_stamps else -1 for v in range(max_value + 1)]
    else:
        return [dp[v] if dp[v] < INF else -1 for v in range(max_value + 1)]


def postage_stamp_reachable(denominations: List[int], max_stamps: int) -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
    """
    Find all postage values reachable using at most `max_stamps` stamps
    of the given denominations.
    
    Returns (reachable_values, highest_reachable_contiguous).
    The 'h-value' or 'n-value' is the largest n such that all values
    1..n are representable.
    """
    # Upper bound: max_stamps * max(denominations)
    upper = max_stamps * max(denominations)
    result = min_stamps(denominations, upper, max_stamps)

    reachable = [v for v in range(upper + 1) if result[v] != -1]

    # Find the largest contiguous value from 1
    h_value = 0
    for v in range(1, upper + 1):
        if result[v] != -1:
            h_value = v
        else:
            break

    return reachable, h_value


def frobenius_number(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    """
    For two coprime denominations a and b, the Frobenius number (largest value
    that cannot be represented) is a*b - a - b.
    
    If gcd(a,b) != 1, there are infinitely many non-representable values.
    """
    if gcd(a, b) != 1:
        raise ValueError(f"Denominations {a} and {b} must be coprime for a finite Frobenius number")
    return a * b - a - b


def frobenius_number_general(denominations: List[int], limit: int = 10000) -> int:
    """
    For a general set of denominations (must have gcd = 1),
    find the Frobenius number by DP.
    """
    from functools import reduce
    overall_gcd = reduce(gcd, denominations)
    if overall_gcd != 1:
        raise ValueError("GCD of all denominations must be 1 for a finite Frobenius number")

    # No limit on number of stamps (unlimited supply)
    result = min_stamps(denominations, limit, max_stamps=None)

    frobenius = -1
    for v in range(limit, -1, -1):
        if result[v] == -1:
            frobenius = v
            break

    return frobenius

    """Pretty-print a table of reachable values."""

The Wikipedia article on "Postage stamp" describes what postage stamps are — their history, usage, types, and characteristics. It's an encyclopedic article, not a programming task.

However, there **is** a well-known computer science problem called the **"Postage Stamp Problem"** (also known as the **Frobenius problem** or **stamp problem**), which is:

> Given a set of stamp denominations and a limit on how many stamps you can use (say `k` stamps), find the largest contiguous range of postage values (starting from 1) that can be made.

Or in another common formulation:

> Given stamp denominations and a maximum number of stamps `k`, find the highest value `h` such that every value from 1 to `h` can be formed.

Since you asked me to "do whatever the content describes" and produce code, I'll implement the **Postage Stamp Problem** in Python:

```python
def postage_stamp_problem(denominations, max_stamps):
    """
    Postage Stamp Problem (Sylvester-Frobenius variant):
    
    Given a set of stamp denominations and a maximum number of stamps
    you can use (max_stamps), find the largest value h such that every
    integer value from 1 to h can be represented using at most max_stamps
    stamps of the given denominations.
    
    Uses dynamic programming approach.
    
    Args:
        denominations: list of positive integer stamp denominations
        max_stamps: maximum number of stamps that can be used
    
    Returns:
        h: the largest value such that all values 1..h are achievable
    """
    if not denominations or max_stamps <= 0:
        return 0
    
    # Upper bound: max denomination * max stamps
    max_value = max(denominations) * max_stamps
    
    # dp[v] = minimum number of stamps needed to make value v
    # Initialize with infinity (unreachable)
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_value + 1)
    dp[0] = 0  # 0 value requires 0 stamps
    
    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 <= max_stamps:
                dp[v] = min(dp[v], dp[v - d] + 1)
    
    # Find the largest h such that all values 1..h are achievable
    h = 0
    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        if dp[v] <= max_stamps:
            h = v
        else:
            break
    
    return h


def print_stamp_table(denominations, max_stamps):
    """
    Print a table showing, for each value, the minimum stamps needed
    and whether it's achievable.
    """
    max_value = max(denominations) * max_stamps
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_value + 1)
    dp[0] = 0
    
    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 <= max_stamps:
                dp[v] = min(dp[v], dp[v - d] + 1)
    
    h = postage_stamp_problem(denominations, max_stamps)
    
    print(f"Denominations: {denominations}")
    print(f"Max stamps allowed: {max_stamps}")
    print(f"Largest consecutive value (h): {h}")
    print()
    print(f"{'Value':>6} {'Min Stamps':>12} {'Achievable':>12}")
    print("-" * 32)
    
    for v in range(1, min(h + 5, max_value + 1)):
        stamps = dp[v] if dp[v] != INF else None
        achievable = "Yes" if stamps is not None else "No"
        stamps_str = str(stamps) if stamps is not None else "—"
        marker = " <-- h" if v == h else ""
        print(f"{v:>6} {stamps_str:>12} {achievable:>12}{marker}")


def find_representation(denominations, max_stamps, target):
    """
    Find which stamps to use to make a target value (if possible).
    Returns a list of stamp denominations used, or None if not possible.
    """
    max_value = target
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_value + 1)
    parent = [-1] * (max_value + 1)
    dp[0] = 0
    
    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 <= max_stamps:
                if dp[v - d] + 1 < dp[v]:
                    dp[v] = dp[v - d] + 1
                    parent[v] = d
    
    if dp[target] == INF:
        return None
    
    # Reconstruct
    result = []
    v = target
    while v > 0:
        result.append(parent[v])
        v -= parent[v]
    
    return sorted(result)


# ============================================
# Examples
# ============================================
if __name__ == "__main__":

    denominations = [1, 5, 10, 25]
    max_stamps = 4
    h = postage_stamp_problem(denominations, max_stamps)
    print(f"Denominations: {denominations}, Max stamps: {max_stamps}")

I'm familiar with this Stack Overflow question. It asks about **how to use the new `ChatOpenAI` (or OpenAI chat models) with LangChain**, specifically around the time when OpenAI released the `gpt-3.5-turbo` model and the ChatCompletion API. The question (Q&A ID 74619456) is about **how to use OpenAI's Chat Completion API with LangChain** — essentially how to integrate `gpt-3.5-turbo` or `gpt-4` chat models into LangChain.

Here is the code showing how to do this properly with LangChain:

## Using Chat Models in LangChain

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import (
    HumanMessage,
    SystemMessage,
    AIMessage,
)

# Initialize the chat model
chat = ChatOpenAI(
    model="gpt-3.5-turbo",
    temperature=0,
    openai_api_key="your-api-key-here",  # or set OPENAI_API_KEY env var
)

# Simple usage with messages
messages = [
    SystemMessage(content="You are a helpful assistant that translates English to French."),
    HumanMessage(content="Translate this sentence: I love programming."),
]

response = chat.invoke(messages)
print(response.content)
```

## Using with Prompt Templates and Chains

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain.chains import LLMChain

chat = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-3.5-turbo", temperature=0)

# Using ChatPromptTemplate
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant that translates {input_language} to {output_language}."),
    ("human", "{text}"),
])

# Modern approach using LCEL (LangChain Expression Language)
chain = prompt | chat

response = chain.invoke({
    "input_language": "English",
    "output_language": "French",
    "text": "I love programming.",
})

print(response.content)
```

## Using the older LLMChain style (still works)

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain.chains import LLMChain

chat = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-3.5-turbo", temperature=0)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Tell me a joke about {topic}")

chain = LLMChain(llm=chat, prompt=prompt)

result = chain.run(topic="bears")
print(result)
```

## Streaming

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage

chat = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-3.5-turbo", temperature=0, streaming=True)

for chunk in chat.stream([HumanMessage(content="Write a poem about AI")]):
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)
```

## Using with GPT-4

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(
    model="gpt-4",
    temperature=0,
)

response = chat.invoke("What is the meaning of life?")
print(response.content)
```

### Key points:

1. **`ChatOpenAI`** is the class for chat models (`gpt-3.5-turbo`, `gpt-4`, etc.) — it uses the `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint.
2. **`OpenAI`** (from `langchain_openai`) is for the older completion models like `text-davinci-003` — it uses the `/v1/completions` endpoint (now largely deprecated).
3. Use `HumanMessage`, `SystemMessage`, `AIMessage` to construct conversation history.
4. The modern package is `langchain_openai` (install via `pip install langchain-openai`). The older import path `from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI` still works but is deprecated.

### Installation:

```bash
pip install langchain langchain-openai
```

The Wikipedia article on "Postage stamp" describes what postage stamps are — their history, design, usage, and related topics. It's an encyclopedia article, not a programming task.

However, there is a well-known **computer science / mathematics problem** called the **"Postage Stamp Problem"** (also known as the **Frobenius problem** or **coin problem** variant):

> Given `n` stamp denominations and the ability to use at most `k` stamps, what is the largest contiguous postage value (starting from 1) that can be formed?

This is sometimes called the **"Postage Stamp Problem"** or **"Stamp Problem"** and is referenced on Wikipedia.

```python
"""
Postage Stamp Problem (also called the Stamp Problem / Local Postage Stamp Problem)

Given a set of stamp denominations and a maximum number of stamps you can use (k),
find the largest value h such that every integer value from 1 to h can be formed
using at most k stamps.

This is also known as the "h-range" of the stamp denominations with limit k.
"""

def postage_stamp_problem(denominations, max_stamps):
    """
    Solve the Postage Stamp Problem using dynamic programming.
    
    Parameters:
        denominations: list of positive integers representing stamp denominations
        max_stamps: maximum number of stamps that can be used (k)
    
    Returns:
        h: the largest integer such that all values 1..h can be represented
           using at most max_stamps stamps from the given denominations
    """
    if not denominations or max_stamps <= 0:
        return 0
    
    # We need an upper bound for our search. The maximum possible value
    # is max_stamps * max(denominations)
    max_val = max_stamps * max(denominations)
    
    # dp[v] = minimum number of stamps needed to make value v
    # Initialize with infinity (impossible)
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_val + 1)
    dp[0] = 0  # 0 stamps needed to make value 0
    
    # Fill the DP table
    for v in range(1, max_val + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 < dp[v]:
                dp[v] = dp[v - d] + 1
    
    # Find the largest h such that all values 1..h can be made with <= max_stamps
    h = 0
    for v in range(1, max_val + 1):
        if dp[v] <= max_stamps:
            h = v
        else:
            break
    
    return h


def find_all_representable(denominations, max_stamps):
    """
    Find all values representable using at most max_stamps stamps.
    Returns a set of achievable values and the minimum stamps needed for each.
    """
    max_val = max_stamps * max(denominations)
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_val + 1)
    dp[0] = 0
    
    for v in range(1, max_val + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 < dp[v]:
                dp[v] = dp[v - d] + 1
    
    representable = {v: dp[v] for v in range(1, max_val + 1) if dp[v] <= max_stamps}
    return representable


def print_stamp_table(denominations, max_stamps):
    """Print a table showing how each value can be achieved."""
    h = postage_stamp_problem(denominations, max_stamps)
    max_val = max_stamps * max(denominations)
    
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_val + 1)
    dp[0] = 0
    parent = [None] * (max_val + 1)
    
    for v in range(1, max_val + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 < dp[v]:
                dp[v] = dp[v - d] + 1
                parent[v] = d
    
    print(f"Denominations: {denominations}")
    print(f"Max stamps allowed: {max_stamps}")
    print(f"h-range (largest contiguous value from 1): {h}")
    print()
    print(f"{'Value':>6} {'Min Stamps':>11} {'Combination':>20}")
    print("-" * 40)
    
    for v in range(1, h + 5):  # Show a few beyond h
        if dp[v] <= max_stamps:
            # Reconstruct the combination
            combo = []
            remaining = v
            while remaining > 0:
                combo.append(parent[remaining])
                remaining -= parent[remaining]
            combo.sort()
            status = "  ✓" if v <= h else ""
            print(f"{v:>6} {dp[v]:>11} {str(combo):>20}{status}")
        else:
            print(f"{v:>6} {'impossible':>11} {'---':>20}  ← gap")
            if v > h + 3:
                break


# =====================================================================
# Examples

if __name__ == "__main__":

    # But 7 needs only if we have k>=3: 3+3+1=7 (3 stamps) ✓
    # 8=3+3+1+1 needs 4 stamps > 3, but 8 can't be done
"""

The Wikipedia article on "Postage stamp" describes what postage stamps are — their history, usage, types, and characteristics. It's an encyclopedia article, not a coding challenge.

However, there **is** a well-known computer science problem called the **"Postage Stamp Problem"** (also known as the **Stamp Problem** or **Chicken McNugget theorem** variant), which is often associated with this topic:

> **Given a set of stamp denominations and a maximum number of stamps you can use, find the largest contiguous postage value (starting from 1) that can be formed.**

This is sometimes called the **"Postage Stamp Problem" / "Frobenius" / "n-stamp problem"**.

Let me produce code for the **Postage Stamp Problem**:

```python
"""
Postage Stamp Problem (also known as the Stamp Basis Problem):

Given:
  - A set of stamp denominations (e.g., [1, 5, 10, 25])
  - A maximum number of stamps you can use (max_stamps)

Find:
  The largest value h such that every integer value from 1 to h
  can be formed using at most max_stamps stamps of the given denominations.
  This value h is called the "h-range" of the stamp basis.

Also computes: for each reachable value, the minimum number of stamps needed.
"""

from typing import List, Tuple, Dict


def min_stamps_needed(denominations: List[int], max_value: int) -> List[int]:
    """
    Classic DP: compute minimum number of stamps to make each value from 0..max_value.
    Returns a list where result[v] = minimum stamps needed for value v, or float('inf') if impossible.
    """
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (max_value + 1)
    dp[0] = 0

    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        for d in denominations:
            if d <= v and dp[v - d] + 1 < dp[v]:
                dp[v] = dp[v - d] + 1

    return dp


def postage_stamp_h_range(denominations: List[int], max_stamps: int) -> Tuple[int, Dict[int, int]]:
    """
    Find the h-range: the largest h such that every value 1..h can be
    formed using at most max_stamps stamps from the given denominations.

    Returns:
        (h_range, details) where details maps each value -> minimum stamps needed
    """
    # Upper bound: max_stamps * max(denominations)
    if not denominations:
        return 0, {}

    max_value = max_stamps * max(denominations)
    dp = min_stamps_needed(denominations, max_value)

    # Find h-range: largest contiguous run from 1
    h_range = 0
    details = {}
    for v in range(1, max_value + 1):
        if dp[v] <= max_stamps:
            h_range = v
            details[v] = dp[v]
        else:
            break

    return h_range, details


def print_stamp_table(denominations: List[int], max_stamps: int):
    """Pretty-print the postage stamp problem results."""
    h_range, details = postage_stamp_h_range(denominations, max_stamps)

    print("=" * 60)
    print(f"Postage Stamp Problem")
    print(f"Denominations: {denominations}")
    print(f"Max stamps allowed: {max_stamps}")
    print(f"h-range (largest contiguous value): {h_range}")
    print("=" * 60)
    print(f"{'Value':>8} {'Min Stamps':>12} {'Representation':>30}")
    print("-" * 60)

    for v in range(1, h_range + 1):
        # Reconstruct which stamps are used
        representation = reconstruct(denominations, v, details[v])
        print(f"{v:>8} {details[v]:>12} {representation:>30}")

    # Show the first unreachable value
    next_val = h_range + 1
    max_possible = max_stamps * max(denominations)
    if next_val <= max_possible:
        dp = min_stamps_needed(denominations, next_val)
        if dp[next_val] > max_stamps:
            print(f"\nValue {next_val} requires {dp[next_val] if dp[next_val] != float('inf') else '∞'} stamps (exceeds limit of {max_stamps})")


def reconstruct(denominations: List[int], target: int, max_used: int) -> str:
    """Reconstruct the stamps used to form the target value."""
    denominations_sorted = sorted(denominations, reverse=True)
    result = []

    def backtrack(remaining, stamps_left, start_idx):
        if remaining == 0:
            return True
        if stamps_left == 0 or remaining < 0:

"""

This module models postage stamps and solves the classic Postage Stamp Problem:

class StampType(Enum):
    """Types of postage stamps as described in the Wikipedia article."""
    DEFINITIVE = auto()       # Regular issue stamps for everyday use
    COMMEMORATIVE = auto()    # Issued to honor people, events, anniversaries
    AIRMAIL = auto()          # For airmail postage
    POSTAGE_DUE = auto()      # Applied to mail with insufficient postage
    REVENUE = auto()          # For tax collection, not postage
    SEMIPOSTAL = auto()       # Charity stamps with surcharge

    COIL = auto()             # Issued in rolls for vending machines
    BOOKLET = auto()          # Issued in small booklets
    SELF_ADHESIVE = auto()    # Modern self-stick stamps
    EXPRESS = auto()          # For express/special delivery


class StampCondition(Enum):
    """Philatelic condition grades."""
    SUPERB = "Superb"
    EXTREMELY_FINE = "Extremely Fine"
    VERY_FINE = "Very Fine"
    FINE = "Fine"
    VERY_GOOD = "Very Good"
    GOOD = "Good"
    AVERAGE = "Average"
    POOR = "Poor"


class CancellationType(Enum):

    MINT_NEVER_HINGED = "Mint Never Hinged"
    MINT_HINGED = "Mint Hinged"
    USED = "Used"

    FIRST_DAY_COVER = "First Day Cover"
    PEN_CANCELLED = "Pen Cancelled"


@dataclass
class Perforation:
    """
    Stamp perforations - the small holes punched between stamps
    to allow easy separation. First introduced by Henry Archer in 1848,
    adopted by Great Britain in 1854.
    
    Gauge is measured as the number of perforation holes in 2 centimeters.
    """
    gauge_horizontal: float  # perf gauge (holes per 2cm)
    gauge_vertical: float
    
    def __str__(self):
        if self.gauge_horizontal == self.gauge_vertical:
            return f"Perf {self.gauge_horizontal}"
        return f"Perf {self.gauge_horizontal} × {self.gauge_vertical}"
    
    @property
    def is_imperforate(self) -> bool:
        return self.gauge_horizontal == 0 and self.gauge_vertical == 0
    
    @staticmethod
    def imperforate() -> 'Perforation':
        """Early stamps (like the Penny Black) were imperforate."""
        return Perforation(0, 0)

# RFC 9110 — HTTP Semantics

## What It Is

RFC 9110, titled **"HTTP Semantics"**, is an **Internet Standards Track** document (STD 97) published in **June 2022** by the IETF. It was edited by **Roy T. Fielding** (Adobe), **Mark Nottingham** (Fastly), and **Julian Reschke** (greenbytes).

It defines the **version-independent semantics** of the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) — that is, the core protocol elements, meanings, and mechanisms that are shared across all versions of HTTP (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, etc.).

## Relationship to Other RFCs

- **Obsoletes:** RFCs 2818, 7230 (portions), 7231, 7232, 7233, 7235, 7538, 7615, and 7694. This means it consolidates and supersedes the previously separate documents covering HTTP methods, status codes, conditional requests, range requests, authentication, HTTPS, and more.
- **Updates:** RFC 3864 (the registration procedures for message header fields).

## Scope

The document covers the **overall architecture of HTTP**, establishes **common terminology**, and defines the **"http" and "https" URI schemes**. It is explicitly a **stateless application-level protocol** specification for distributed, collaborative, hypertext information systems.

## Key Sections

1. **Section 3 – Terminology and Core Concepts:** Defines resources, representations, connections, clients, servers, user agents, origin servers, intermediaries (proxies, gateways), and caches.

2. **Section 4 – Identifiers in HTTP:** Covers URI references, the `http` and `https` URI schemes, normalization/comparison rules, deprecation of `userinfo` in HTTP URIs, and authoritative access (including `https` certificate verification).

3. **Section 5 – Fields:** Defines the general framework for HTTP header and trailer fields — field names, field lines vs. combined field values, field ordering, common ABNF rules (tokens, quoted strings, parameters, date/time formats, the `#rule` list syntax).

4. **Section 8 – Representation Data and Metadata:** Covers `Content-Type` (media types, charsets, multipart types), `Content-Encoding` (gzip, deflate, compress), `Content-Language`, `Content-Length`, `Content-Location`, and **validator fields** (`ETag`, `Last-Modified`, weak vs. strong validators).

5. **Section 9 – Methods:** Defines the standard HTTP methods — **GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, DELETE, CONNECT, OPTIONS, TRACE** — and their properties (safe, idempotent, cacheable).

6. **Section 11 – HTTP Authentication:** Defines the authentication framework: schemes, challenges, credentials, protection spaces (realms), and the header fields `WWW-Authenticate`, `Authorization`, `Authentication-Info`, `Proxy-Authenticate`, `Proxy-Authorization`, and `Proxy-Authentication-Info`.

7. **Section 12 – Content Negotiation:** Covers proactive and reactive negotiation, quality values, and the negotiation fields `Accept`, `Accept-Charset`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Accept-Language`, and `Vary`.

8. **Section 13 – Conditional Requests:** Defines precondition header fields (`If-Match`, `If-None-Match`, `If-Modified-Since`, `If-Unmodified-Since`, `If-Range`) and their evaluation precedence.

9. **Section 14 – Range Requests:** Defines range units (byte ranges), the `Range` and `Accept-Ranges` headers, `Content-Range`, partial PUT, and the `multipart/byteranges` media type.

10. **Section 15 – Status Codes:** The full registry of status codes organized by class: **1xx Informational**, **2xx Successful**, **3xx Redirection**, **4xx Client Error**, **5xx Server Error**. Notable entries include `308 Permanent Redirect` (absorbed from RFC 7538), `421 Misdirected Request`, and `422 Unprocessable Content`.

11. **Section 16 – Extending HTTP:** Describes extensibility mechanisms and IANA registries for methods, status codes, fields, authentication schemes, range units, content codings, and upgrade tokens.

12. **Section 17 – Security Considerations:** Addresses risks including intermediary attacks, path/command injection, protocol element length attacks, shared-dictionary compression attacks, and disclosure of personal/sensitive information.

## Key Takeaway

RFC 9110 is the **single, consolidated, authoritative specification for HTTP semantics** — separating the *meaning* of HTTP from its *wire format* (which is version-specific and covered by companion RFCs such as RFC 9112 for HTTP/1.1 messaging and RFC 9113 for HTTP/2).

# RFC 9110 – HTTP Semantics

## What it is

RFC 9110, titled **"HTTP Semantics"**, was published in June 2022. It is authored by Roy Fielding, Mark Nottingham, and Julian Reschke. It obsoletes the earlier RFCs 2818, 7230, 7231, 7232, 7233, 7235, and 7538, consolidating and updating their content.

RFC 9110 is part of a family of three RFCs that together replaced the older HTTP/1.1 specification suite:

- **RFC 9110** – HTTP Semantics (version-independent semantics)
- **RFC 9111** – HTTP Caching
- **RFC 9112** – HTTP/1.1

## Scope

RFC 9110 defines the **version-independent** semantics of HTTP: the concepts, architecture, and protocol elements that are shared across all versions of HTTP (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3). It separates the *what* of HTTP (semantics) from the *how* (wire format), allowing versions like HTTP/2 (RFC 9113) and HTTP/3 (RFC 9114) to reference a single semantics document.

It covers:

- The abstract architecture of HTTP (client, server, intermediaries, origin servers, proxies, gateways/tunnels)
- Resources, URIs, and the concept of representations
- The overall message model (requests, responses, headers, trailers, content)
- All standard request methods and response status codes
- Content negotiation
- Conditional requests
- Range requests
- Authentication framework

## Key Sections (by topic area)

### 1. Architecture & Terminology (Sections 1–3)
- Defines the roles: **user agent, origin server, proxy, gateway, tunnel**.
- Introduces the concepts of **resources**, **representations**, and **selected representation**.
- Defines **intermediaries** (forward proxies, reverse proxies).

### 2. Identifiers in HTTP (Section 4)
- URI schemes `http` and `https`, their default ports (80 and 443), and authority.
- Normalization and comparison of URIs for determining origin.

### 3. Fields (Section 5)
- General framework for **header fields** and **trailer fields**.
- Field ordering, combining rules for fields with list-based values.

### 4. Message Abstraction (Section 6)
- Defines **content** (the payload body after any transfer coding is removed), distinct from the HTTP/1.1 concept of "message body".
- Trailer fields, framing, and completeness.

### 5. Request Methods (Section 9)
Defines the standard methods and their properties (safe, idempotent, cacheable):

| Method | Safe | Idempotent | Cacheable |
|---------|------|------------|-----------|
| GET | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| HEAD | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| POST | ✘ | ✘ | Conditional |
| PUT | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ |
| DELETE | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ |
| CONNECT | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |
| OPTIONS | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ |
| TRACE | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ |

### 6. Response Status Codes (Section 15)
All status code classes and individual codes:
- **1xx** Informational (e.g., 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols)
- **2xx** Successful (e.g., 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content)
- **3xx** Redirection (e.g., 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 307 Temporary Redirect, 308 Permanent Redirect — absorbing RFC 7538)
- **4xx** Client Error (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 405 Method Not Allowed)
- **5xx** Server Error (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable)

### 7. Content Negotiation (Sections 12)
- **Proactive negotiation** (server-driven): client sends preferences via `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Accept-Charset`.
- **Reactive negotiation** (agent-driven): server provides a list of alternatives (e.g., 300 Multiple Choices).

### 8. Conditional Requests (Section 13)
- Validators: **ETag** (strong and weak entity tags) and **Last-Modified**.
- Precondition headers: `If-Match`, `If-None-Match`, `If-Modified-Since`, `If-Unmodified-Since`, `If-Range`.
- Evaluation precedence rules.

### 9. Range Requests (Section 14)
- The `Range` header and `Accept-Ranges`.
- 206 Partial Content and 416 Range Not Satisfiable.
- Multipart/byteranges media type.

### 10. Authentication (Section 11)
- The general **challenge-response** authentication framework.
- `WWW-Authenticate`, `Authorization`, `Proxy-Authenticate`, `Proxy-Authorization` headers.
- 401 Unauthorized and 407 Proxy Authentication Required responses.
- Protection spaces and the concept of realms.

## Key Design Choices

- **Version independence**: By factoring out semantics from wire format, HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 simply reference RFC 9110 rather than redefining methods, status codes, and header semantics.
- **Consolidation**: Merging what was previously spread across RFCs 7230–7235 plus 7538 reduces cross-reference complexity.
- **Clarifications and corrections**: Many ambiguities from the older RFCs were resolved (e.g., clearer rules about content in responses to HEAD, clearer definitions of when validators must be sent).

# Postage Stamp — Summary of the Wikipedia Article

The Wikipedia article on **Postage stamp** covers the history, purpose, production, and collecting of postage stamps. Here are the key facts:

## Definition
A **postage stamp** is a small piece of paper issued by a postal authority that is affixed to mail to indicate that postage has been paid. It typically displays a denomination (monetary value), the issuing country's name, and a design or image.

## History
- The first adhesive postage stamp was the **Penny Black**, issued by **Great Britain** on **May 1, 1840**. It featured a profile of **Queen Victoria**.
- The system was proposed by **Sir Rowland Hill** in his 1837 pamphlet *Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability*. Hill advocated for a uniform penny postage rate regardless of distance within the country.
- Before stamps, postage was typically paid by the recipient, and rates varied by distance.
- The **Two Penny Blue** was issued shortly after the Penny Black.
- Other countries quickly adopted the idea: **Brazil** (1843, the Bull's Eye stamps), **Switzerland** (Zurich cantonal stamps, 1843), and the **United States** (1847, featuring Benjamin Franklin and George Washington).

## Design & Production
- Stamps are printed using methods including **intaglio (engraving)**, **lithography**, **photogravure**, and **offset printing**.
- Security features such as **watermarks**, **perforations**, and special paper or inks are used to prevent counterfeiting.
- **Perforations** were introduced by Henry Archer in the 1850s; early stamps had to be cut with scissors.
- Stamps can be **definitive** (standard, everyday use, printed in large quantities) or **commemorative** (issued for limited periods to honor events, people, or themes).

## Types of Stamps
- **Definitive stamps** — Regular issue, often small and simple in design.
- **Commemorative stamps** — Special issues marking events, anniversaries, famous persons, etc.
- **Airmail stamps** — For mail carried by air.
- **Postage due stamps** — Indicating insufficient postage was paid.
- **Semi-postal (charity) stamps** — Include a surcharge for a charitable cause.
- **Revenue stamps** — Used for tax purposes (distinct but related).
- **Miniature sheets / souvenir sheets** — Stamps issued on small decorative sheets.

## Stamp Collecting (Philately)
- **Philately** is the study and collecting of stamps; the term was coined by **Georges Herpin** in 1864.
- It is one of the world's most popular hobbies.
- Stamps are collected in various conditions: **mint** (unused, with original gum) and **used** (cancelled/postmarked).
- Famous rarities include the **British Guiana 1c Magenta** (one of the world's most valuable stamps), the **Inverted Jenny** (a U.S. stamp with an upside-down airplane), and the **Treskilling Yellow** (a Swedish color error).
- Catalogs such as **Scott**, **Stanley Gibbons**, **Michel**, and **Yvert et Tellier** are used to identify and value stamps.

## Modern Developments
- The use of postage stamps has declined with the rise of **metered mail**, **digital postage** (e.g., stamps.com), and declining letter volumes due to email and electronic communication.
- Some countries have issued **self-adhesive stamps** (replacing lick-and-stick gummed stamps).
- **Forever stamps** (notably in the U.S., introduced in 2007) retain their validity regardless of future rate increases.
- Some postal authorities have issued novelty stamps on unusual materials (wood, silk, etc.) or with special features (scented, holographic, embroidered).

## Universal Postal Union (UPU)
- The UPU, established in 1874, coordinates postal policies among member nations and has rules regarding stamp issuance, including the requirement that stamps bear the issuing country's name (with Great Britain being the sole exception, using the monarch's silhouette instead, as the originator of the postage stamp).

This covers the major content of the Wikipedia article on postage stamps.
